question_text: The integrating factor of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+y=\frac{1+y}{x}{/tex} is:

options.text: {tex}\frac{x}{e^{x}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{e^{x}}{x}{/tex}
options.explanation: We have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+y=\frac{1+y}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{x}+\frac{y(1-x)}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}-\left(\frac{1-x}{x}\right) y=\frac{1}{x}{/tex} This is a linear differential equation. On comparing it with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex} , we get {tex}P=\frac{-(1-x)}{x}, Q=\frac{1}{x}{/tex} I.F. = {tex}e^{\int P d x}=e^{-\int \frac{1-x}{x} d x}{/tex} {tex}=e^{x-\log x}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{e^{x}}{x}{/tex}

options.text: xe x
e x

question_html: Solve the differential equation {tex}y e^{\frac{x}{y}} d x=\left(x e^{\frac{x}{y}}+y^{2}\right) d y(y \neq 0){/tex}
question_text: Solve the differential equation {tex}y e^{\frac{x}{y}} d x=\left(x e^{\frac{x}{y}}+y^{2}\right) d y(y \neq 0){/tex}
explanation: It is given that {tex}\text { ye }^{\frac{x}{y}} d x=\left(x e^{\frac{x}{y}}+y^{2}\right) d y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \text { ye }^{\frac{x}{y}} \frac{d x}{d y}=x e^{\frac{x}{y}}+y^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\frac{x}{y}}\left[y \cdot \frac{d x}{d y}-x\right]=y^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow e^{\frac{x}{y}} \cdot \frac{\left[y \cdot \frac{d x}{d y}-x\right]}{y^{2}}=1{/tex} .......(i) Let {tex}e^{\frac{x}{y}}=z{/tex} Differentiating it w.r.t. y, we get, {tex}\frac{d}{d y}\left(e^{\frac{x}{y}}\right)=\frac{d z}{d y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow e^{\frac{x}{y}} \cdot \frac{d}{d y}\left(\frac{x}{y}\right)=\frac{d z}{d y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow e^{\frac{x}{y}} \cdot\left[\frac{y \cdot \frac{d x}{d y}-x}{y^{2}}\right]=\frac{d z}{d y}{/tex} ......(ii) From equation (i) and equation (ii), we get, {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{z}}{\mathrm{dy}}=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dz = dy On integrating both sides, we get, z = y + C {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{y}}}=\mathrm{y}+\mathrm{C}{/tex}
solution: It is given that {tex}\text { ye }^{\frac{x}{y}} d x=\left(x e^{\frac{x}{y}}+y^{2}\right) d y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \text { ye }^{\frac{x}{y}} \frac{d x}{d y}=x e^{\frac{x}{y}}+y^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\frac{x}{y}}\left[y \cdot \frac{d x}{d y}-x\right]=y^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow e^{\frac{x}{y}} \cdot \frac{\left[y \cdot \frac{d x}{d y}-x\right]}{y^{2}}=1{/tex} .......(i) Let {tex}e^{\frac{x}{y}}=z{/tex} Differentiating it w.r.t. y, we get, {tex}\frac{d}{d y}\left(e^{\frac{x}{y}}\right)=\frac{d z}{d y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow e^{\frac{x}{y}} \cdot \frac{d}{d y}\left(\frac{x}{y}\right)=\frac{d z}{d y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow e^{\frac{x}{y}} \cdot\left[\frac{y \cdot \frac{d x}{d y}-x}{y^{2}}\right]=\frac{d z}{d y}{/tex} ......(ii) From equation (i) and equation (ii), we get, {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{z}}{\mathrm{dy}}=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dz = dy On integrating both sides, we get, z = y + C {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{y}}}=\mathrm{y}+\mathrm{C}{/tex}
solution_html:
It is given that {tex}\text { ye }^{\frac{x}{y}} d x=\left(x e^{\frac{x}{y}}+y^{2}\right) d y{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \text { ye }^{\frac{x}{y}} \frac{d x}{d y}=x e^{\frac{x}{y}}+y^{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\frac{x}{y}}\left[y \cdot \frac{d x}{d y}-x\right]=y^{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow e^{\frac{x}{y}} \cdot \frac{\left[y \cdot \frac{d x}{d y}-x\right]}{y^{2}}=1{/tex} .......(i)
Let {tex}e^{\frac{x}{y}}=z{/tex} 
Differentiating it w.r.t. y, we get,
{tex}\frac{d}{d y}\left(e^{\frac{x}{y}}\right)=\frac{d z}{d y}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow e^{\frac{x}{y}} \cdot \frac{d}{d y}\left(\frac{x}{y}\right)=\frac{d z}{d y}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow e^{\frac{x}{y}} \cdot\left[\frac{y \cdot \frac{d x}{d y}-x}{y^{2}}\right]=\frac{d z}{d y}{/tex} ......(ii)
From equation (i) and equation (ii), we get,
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{z}}{\mathrm{dy}}=1{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dz = dy
On integrating both sides, we get,
z = y + C
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{y}}}=\mathrm{y}+\mathrm{C}{/tex}

question_html: Solve the differential equation {tex}\left(\tan ^{-1} y-x\right) d y=\left(1+y^{2}\right) d x{/tex}
question_text: Solve the differential equation {tex}\left(\tan ^{-1} y-x\right) d y=\left(1+y^{2}\right) d x{/tex}
explanation: The given differential equation can be written as {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+\frac{x}{1+y^{2}}=\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} .......(i) Now (i) is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+\mathrm{P}_{1} x=\mathrm{Q}_{1}{/tex} where, {tex}P_{1}=\frac{1}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} and {tex}Q_{1}=\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} Therefore, I.F = {tex}e^{\int \frac{1}{1+y^{2}} d y}=e^{\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is {tex}x e^{\tan ^{-1} y}=\int\left(\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}\right) e^{\tan ^{-1} y} d y+C{/tex} ......(ii) Let {tex}\mathrm{I}=\int\left(\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}\right) e^{\tan ^{-1} y} d y{/tex} Substituting tan -1 y = t so that {tex}\left(\frac{1}{1+y^{2}}\right) d y=d t{/tex} , we get {tex}\mathrm{I}=\int t e^{t} d t=t e^{t}-\int 1 . e^{t} d t=t e^{t}-e^{t}=e^{t}(t-1){/tex} or {tex}\mathrm{I}=e^{\tan ^{-1} y}\left(\tan ^{-1} y-1\right){/tex} Substituting the value of I in equation (ii), we get {tex}x \cdot e^{\tan ^{-1} y}=e^{\tan ^{-1} y}\left(\tan ^{-1} y-1\right)+C{/tex} or {tex}x=\left(\tan ^{-1} y-1\right)+C e^{-\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} which is the general solution of the given differential equation.
solution: The given differential equation can be written as {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+\frac{x}{1+y^{2}}=\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} .......(i) Now (i) is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+\mathrm{P}_{1} x=\mathrm{Q}_{1}{/tex} where, {tex}P_{1}=\frac{1}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} and {tex}Q_{1}=\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} Therefore, I.F = {tex}e^{\int \frac{1}{1+y^{2}} d y}=e^{\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is {tex}x e^{\tan ^{-1} y}=\int\left(\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}\right) e^{\tan ^{-1} y} d y+C{/tex} ......(ii) Let {tex}\mathrm{I}=\int\left(\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}\right) e^{\tan ^{-1} y} d y{/tex} Substituting tan -1 y = t so that {tex}\left(\frac{1}{1+y^{2}}\right) d y=d t{/tex} , we get {tex}\mathrm{I}=\int t e^{t} d t=t e^{t}-\int 1 . e^{t} d t=t e^{t}-e^{t}=e^{t}(t-1){/tex} or {tex}\mathrm{I}=e^{\tan ^{-1} y}\left(\tan ^{-1} y-1\right){/tex} Substituting the value of I in equation (ii), we get {tex}x \cdot e^{\tan ^{-1} y}=e^{\tan ^{-1} y}\left(\tan ^{-1} y-1\right)+C{/tex} or {tex}x=\left(\tan ^{-1} y-1\right)+C e^{-\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} which is the general solution of the given differential equation.
solution_html:
The given differential equation can be written as
{tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+\frac{x}{1+y^{2}}=\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} .......(i)
Now (i) is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+\mathrm{P}_{1} x=\mathrm{Q}_{1}{/tex} 
where, {tex}P_{1}=\frac{1}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} and {tex}Q_{1}=\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}{/tex} 
Therefore, I.F = {tex}e^{\int \frac{1}{1+y^{2}} d y}=e^{\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} 
Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is
{tex}x e^{\tan ^{-1} y}=\int\left(\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}\right) e^{\tan ^{-1} y} d y+C{/tex} ......(ii)
Let {tex}\mathrm{I}=\int\left(\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}\right) e^{\tan ^{-1} y} d y{/tex} 
Substituting tan-1 y = t so that {tex}\left(\frac{1}{1+y^{2}}\right) d y=d t{/tex}, we get
{tex}\mathrm{I}=\int t e^{t} d t=t e^{t}-\int 1 . e^{t} d t=t e^{t}-e^{t}=e^{t}(t-1){/tex} 
or {tex}\mathrm{I}=e^{\tan ^{-1} y}\left(\tan ^{-1} y-1\right){/tex} 
Substituting the value of I in equation (ii), we get 
{tex}x \cdot e^{\tan ^{-1} y}=e^{\tan ^{-1} y}\left(\tan ^{-1} y-1\right)+C{/tex} 
or {tex}x=\left(\tan ^{-1} y-1\right)+C e^{-\tan ^{-1} y}{/tex} 
which is the general solution of the given differential equation.

question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 x y = \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex}, given that y = 0 when x = 1.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 x y = \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} , given that y = 0 when x = 1.
explanation: {tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+2 x y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} Divide both sides by 1 + x 2 {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{2 x y}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\left(1+x^{2}\right) \cdot\left(1+x^{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\left(\frac{2 x }{1+x^{2}}\right) y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^2}{/tex} Comparing with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q, P = {tex}\frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} & Q = {tex}\frac{1}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} Finding Integrating factor: IF = {tex}e^{\int P d x}{/tex} IF = {tex}e^{\int \frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}} d x{/tex} Let 1 + x 2 = t Diff. w.r.t. x 2x = {tex}\frac{dt}{d x} {/tex} dx = {tex}\frac{d t}{2 x}{/tex} Thus, IF = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{2 x}{t} \frac{d t}{2 x}}{/tex} IF = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{d t}{t}}{/tex} IF = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\log |t|}{/tex} IF = t IF = 1 + x 2 Solution of the differential equation: y {tex}\times{/tex} I.F. = {tex}\int {Q\, \times \,I.F\,dx} {/tex} Putting values, y {tex}\times{/tex} (1 + x 2 ) = {tex}{\int {\frac{1}{{(1 + {x^2})^2}}} (1 + {x^2})dx} {/tex} y (1 + x 2 ) = {tex}\int{\frac{1}{{(1 + {x^2})}}} {/tex} dx y (1 + x 2 ) = tan -1 x + C ...(1) Putting that y = 0 and x = 1, 0(1 + 1 2 ) = tan -1 (1) + C 0 = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} + C C = - {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} Putting value of C in eq(1), y(1 + x 2 ) = tan -1 x + C y(1 + x 2 ) = tan -1 x - {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+2 x y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} Divide both sides by 1 + x 2 {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{2 x y}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\left(1+x^{2}\right) \cdot\left(1+x^{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\left(\frac{2 x }{1+x^{2}}\right) y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^2}{/tex} Comparing with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q, P = {tex}\frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} & Q = {tex}\frac{1}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} Finding Integrating factor: IF = {tex}e^{\int P d x}{/tex} IF = {tex}e^{\int \frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}} d x{/tex} Let 1 + x 2 = t Diff. w.r.t. x 2x = {tex}\frac{dt}{d x} {/tex} dx = {tex}\frac{d t}{2 x}{/tex} Thus, IF = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{2 x}{t} \frac{d t}{2 x}}{/tex} IF = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{d t}{t}}{/tex} IF = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\log |t|}{/tex} IF = t IF = 1 + x 2 Solution of the differential equation: y {tex}\times{/tex} I.F. = {tex}\int {Q\, \times \,I.F\,dx} {/tex} Putting values, y {tex}\times{/tex} (1 + x 2 ) = {tex}{\int {\frac{1}{{(1 + {x^2})^2}}} (1 + {x^2})dx} {/tex} y (1 + x 2 ) = {tex}\int{\frac{1}{{(1 + {x^2})}}} {/tex} dx y (1 + x 2 ) = tan -1 x + C ...(1) Putting that y = 0 and x = 1, 0(1 + 1 2 ) = tan -1 (1) + C 0 = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} + C C = - {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} Putting value of C in eq(1), y(1 + x 2 ) = tan -1 x + C y(1 + x 2 ) = tan -1 x - {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+2 x y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}{/tex}
Divide both sides by 1 + x2
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{2 x y}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\left(1+x^{2}\right) \cdot\left(1+x^{2}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\left(\frac{2 x }{1+x^{2}}\right) y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^2}{/tex}
Comparing with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q,
P = {tex}\frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} & Q = {tex}\frac{1}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
Finding Integrating factor:
IF = {tex}e^{\int P d x}{/tex}
IF = {tex}e^{\int \frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}} d x{/tex}
Let 1 + x2 = t
Diff. w.r.t. x
2x = {tex}\frac{dt}{d x} {/tex}
dx = {tex}\frac{d t}{2 x}{/tex}
Thus, IF = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{2 x}{t} \frac{d t}{2 x}}{/tex}
IF = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\int \frac{d t}{t}}{/tex}
IF = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\log |t|}{/tex}
IF = t
IF = 1 + x2
Solution of the differential equation:
y {tex}\times{/tex} I.F. = {tex}\int {Q\, \times \,I.F\,dx} {/tex}
Putting values,
y {tex}\times{/tex} (1 + x2) = {tex}{\int {\frac{1}{{(1 + {x^2})^2}}} (1 + {x^2})dx} {/tex} 
y (1 + x2) = {tex}\int{\frac{1}{{(1 + {x^2})}}} {/tex} dx
y (1 + x2) = tan-1x + C ...(1)
Putting that y = 0 and x = 1,
0(1 + 12) = tan-1(1) + C
0 = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} + C
C = -{tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
Putting value of C in eq(1),
y(1 + x2) = tan-1x + C
y(1 + x2) = tan-1x - {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}

question_html: Find a particular solution of the differential equation {tex}(x+1) \frac{d y}{d x}=2 e^{-y}-1{/tex} given that y = 0 when x = 0.
question_text: Find a particular solution of the differential equation {tex}(x+1) \frac{d y}{d x}=2 e^{-y}-1{/tex} given that y = 0 when x = 0.
explanation: It is given that {tex}(x+1) \frac{d y}{d x}=2 e^{-y}-1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{2 e^{-y}-1}=\frac{d x}{x+1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{e^{y} d y}{2-e^{y}}=\frac{d x}{x+1}{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get, {tex}\int \frac{e^{y} d y}{2-e^{y}}=\log |x+1|+\log C{/tex} .....(i) Let {tex}2-e^{y}=t{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d}{d y}\left(2-e^{y}\right)=\frac{d t}{d y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{dy}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e y dy = -dt Substituting value in equation (i), we get, {tex}\int \frac{-\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{t}}=\log |\mathrm{x}+1|+\log \mathrm{c}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -log|t| = log|C(x+1)| {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -log|2 - e y | = log|C(x + 1)| {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}}=\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{x}+1){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{c}(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} ......(ii) Now, at x = 0 and y = 0, equation (ii) becomes, {tex}\Rightarrow 2-1=\frac{1}{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 1 Now, substituting the value of C in equation (ii), we get, {tex}\Rightarrow 2-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{1}{(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=2-\frac{1}{(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{x}+2-1}{(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{x}+1}{(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\log \left|\frac{2 \mathrm{x}+1}{\mathrm{x}+1}\right|,(\mathrm{x} \neq-1){/tex} Therefore, the required particular solution of the given differential equation is {tex}y=\log \left|\frac{2 x+1}{x+1}\right|,(x \neq-1){/tex}
solution: It is given that {tex}(x+1) \frac{d y}{d x}=2 e^{-y}-1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{2 e^{-y}-1}=\frac{d x}{x+1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{e^{y} d y}{2-e^{y}}=\frac{d x}{x+1}{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get, {tex}\int \frac{e^{y} d y}{2-e^{y}}=\log |x+1|+\log C{/tex} .....(i) Let {tex}2-e^{y}=t{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d}{d y}\left(2-e^{y}\right)=\frac{d t}{d y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{dy}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e y dy = -dt Substituting value in equation (i), we get, {tex}\int \frac{-\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{t}}=\log |\mathrm{x}+1|+\log \mathrm{c}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -log|t| = log|C(x+1)| {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -log|2 - e y | = log|C(x + 1)| {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}}=\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{x}+1){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{c}(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} ......(ii) Now, at x = 0 and y = 0, equation (ii) becomes, {tex}\Rightarrow 2-1=\frac{1}{C}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 1 Now, substituting the value of C in equation (ii), we get, {tex}\Rightarrow 2-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{1}{(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=2-\frac{1}{(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{x}+2-1}{(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{x}+1}{(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\log \left|\frac{2 \mathrm{x}+1}{\mathrm{x}+1}\right|,(\mathrm{x} \neq-1){/tex} Therefore, the required particular solution of the given differential equation is {tex}y=\log \left|\frac{2 x+1}{x+1}\right|,(x \neq-1){/tex}
solution_html:
It is given that {tex}(x+1) \frac{d y}{d x}=2 e^{-y}-1{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{2 e^{-y}-1}=\frac{d x}{x+1}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{e^{y} d y}{2-e^{y}}=\frac{d x}{x+1}{/tex} 
On integrating both sides, we get,
{tex}\int \frac{e^{y} d y}{2-e^{y}}=\log |x+1|+\log C{/tex} .....(i)
Let {tex}2-e^{y}=t{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore \frac{d}{d y}\left(2-e^{y}\right)=\frac{d t}{d y}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{dy}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} eydy = -dt
Substituting value in equation (i), we get,
{tex}\int \frac{-\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{t}}=\log |\mathrm{x}+1|+\log \mathrm{c}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -log|t| = log|C(x+1)|
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -log|2 - ey| = log|C(x + 1)|
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}}=\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{x}+1){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow 2-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{c}(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} ......(ii)
Now, at x = 0 and y = 0, equation (ii) becomes,
{tex}\Rightarrow 2-1=\frac{1}{C}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 1
Now, substituting the value of C in equation (ii), we get,
{tex}\Rightarrow 2-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{1}{(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=2-\frac{1}{(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{x}+2-1}{(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{x}+1}{(\mathrm{x}+1)}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\log \left|\frac{2 \mathrm{x}+1}{\mathrm{x}+1}\right|,(\mathrm{x} \neq-1){/tex} 
Therefore, the required particular solution of the given differential equation is
{tex}y=\log \left|\frac{2 x+1}{x+1}\right|,(x \neq-1){/tex}

question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}-\frac{{y}}{{x}}+\operatorname{cosec}\left(\frac{{y}}{{x}}\right)=0{/tex}; given that {tex}{y}=0{/tex}, when {tex}{x}=1{/tex}.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}-\frac{{y}}{{x}}+\operatorname{cosec}\left(\frac{{y}}{{x}}\right)=0{/tex} ; given that {tex}{y}=0{/tex} , when {tex}{x}=1{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}-\operatorname{cosec} \frac{y}{x}{/tex} Put {tex}{y}={v x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}={v}+{x} \frac{{dv}}{{dx}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {v}+{x} \frac{{d v}}{{d x}}={v}-\operatorname{cosec} {v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-\int \sin {v} {d} {v}=\int \frac{{d} {x}}{{x}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos v=\log |x|+c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos \frac{y}{x}=\log |x|+c{/tex} {tex}{x}={1}, {y}={0} \Rightarrow {c}={1}{/tex} {tex}\Longrightarrow \cos \frac{y}{x}=\log |x|+1{/tex}
solution: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}-\operatorname{cosec} \frac{y}{x}{/tex} Put {tex}{y}={v x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}={v}+{x} \frac{{dv}}{{dx}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {v}+{x} \frac{{d v}}{{d x}}={v}-\operatorname{cosec} {v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-\int \sin {v} {d} {v}=\int \frac{{d} {x}}{{x}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos v=\log |x|+c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos \frac{y}{x}=\log |x|+c{/tex} {tex}{x}={1}, {y}={0} \Rightarrow {c}={1}{/tex} {tex}\Longrightarrow \cos \frac{y}{x}=\log |x|+1{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}-\operatorname{cosec} \frac{y}{x}{/tex}
Put {tex}{y}={v x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}={v}+{x} \frac{{dv}}{{dx}}{/tex}

question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}+ 2y tan x = sin x, given that y = 0 when x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} + 2y tan x = sin x, given that y = 0 when x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} .
explanation: According to the question, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} + 2ytanx = sinx Given equation is a linear differential equation in the form {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} + Py = Q Here, P = 2tanx and Q = sinx Now, Integration Factor(IF) = {tex}e ^ { \int P d x } = e ^ { \int 2 \tan x d x }{/tex} {tex}= e ^ { 2 \int \tan x d x } \\= e ^ { 2 \log | \sec x | }{/tex} {tex}= e ^ { \log \sec ^ { 2 } x } {/tex} {tex}= \sec ^ { 2 } x{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because e ^ { \log f ( x ) } = f ( x ) \right]{/tex} The solution of differential equation is given by {tex}y \cdot ( I F ) = \int ( \operatorname { IF } ) Q d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \sec ^ { 2 } x \cdot \sin x d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \frac { \sin x } { \cos ^ { 2 } x } d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \frac { \sin x } { \cos x } \cdot \frac { 1 } { \cos x } d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \tan x \cdot \sec x d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \sec x + C{/tex} Dividing with sec 2 x on both sides, we get {tex}\Rightarrow y = \frac { 1 } { \sec x } + \frac { C } { \sec ^ { 2 } x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = cosx + C.cos 2 x According to the question, y = 0 when x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} {tex}0 = \cos \frac { \pi } { 3 } + C \cdot \cos ^ { 2 } \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 0 = \frac { 1 } { 2 } + C \cdot \frac { 1 } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { - 1 } { 2 } = \frac { C } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow C = - 2{/tex} Now, y = cosx + C.cos 2 x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = cos x - 2 cos 2 x Therefore, the required particular solution is y = cos x - 2 cos 2 x
solution: According to the question, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} + 2ytanx = sinx Given equation is a linear differential equation in the form {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} + Py = Q Here, P = 2tanx and Q = sinx Now, Integration Factor(IF) = {tex}e ^ { \int P d x } = e ^ { \int 2 \tan x d x }{/tex} {tex}= e ^ { 2 \int \tan x d x } \\= e ^ { 2 \log | \sec x | }{/tex} {tex}= e ^ { \log \sec ^ { 2 } x } {/tex} {tex}= \sec ^ { 2 } x{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because e ^ { \log f ( x ) } = f ( x ) \right]{/tex} The solution of differential equation is given by {tex}y \cdot ( I F ) = \int ( \operatorname { IF } ) Q d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \sec ^ { 2 } x \cdot \sin x d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \frac { \sin x } { \cos ^ { 2 } x } d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \frac { \sin x } { \cos x } \cdot \frac { 1 } { \cos x } d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \tan x \cdot \sec x d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \sec x + C{/tex} Dividing with sec 2 x on both sides, we get {tex}\Rightarrow y = \frac { 1 } { \sec x } + \frac { C } { \sec ^ { 2 } x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = cosx + C.cos 2 x According to the question, y = 0 when x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} {tex}0 = \cos \frac { \pi } { 3 } + C \cdot \cos ^ { 2 } \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 0 = \frac { 1 } { 2 } + C \cdot \frac { 1 } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { - 1 } { 2 } = \frac { C } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow C = - 2{/tex} Now, y = cosx + C.cos 2 x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = cos x - 2 cos 2 x Therefore, the required particular solution is y = cos x - 2 cos 2 x
solution_html:
According to the question, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}+ 2ytanx = sinx
Given equation is a linear differential equation in the form {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex}+ Py = Q
Here, P = 2tanx and Q = sinx
Now, Integration Factor(IF) = {tex}e ^ { \int P d x } = e ^ { \int 2 \tan x d x }{/tex}
{tex}= e ^ { 2 \int \tan x d x } \\= e ^ { 2 \log | \sec x | }{/tex}
{tex}= e ^ { \log \sec ^ { 2 } x } {/tex}
{tex}= \sec ^ { 2 } x{/tex}{tex}\left[ \because e ^ { \log f ( x ) } = f ( x ) \right]{/tex}
The solution of differential equation is given by
{tex}y \cdot ( I F ) = \int ( \operatorname { IF } ) Q d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \sec ^ { 2 } x \cdot \sin x d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \frac { \sin x } { \cos ^ { 2 } x } d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \frac { \sin x } { \cos x } \cdot \frac { 1 } { \cos x } d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \int \tan x \cdot \sec x d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } x = \sec x + C{/tex}
 Dividing with sec2x on both sides, we get
{tex}\Rightarrow y = \frac { 1 } { \sec x } + \frac { C } { \sec ^ { 2 } x }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}y = cosx + C.cos2x
According to the question, y = 0 when x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}
{tex}0 = \cos \frac { \pi } { 3 } + C \cdot \cos ^ { 2 } \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 0 = \frac { 1 } { 2 } + C \cdot \frac { 1 } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { - 1 } { 2 } = \frac { C } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow C = - 2{/tex}
Now, y = cosx + C.cos2x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = cos x - 2 cos2x
Therefore, the required particular solution is y = cos x - 2 cos2x

explanation: Given differential equation is a linear order differential equation with: {tex}P=-2 x, Q=3 x^2 e^{x^2}{/tex} {tex}\text { Integrating Factor }=e^{\int-2 x d x}=e^{-x^2}{/tex} The general solution is: {tex}y \cdot e^{-x^2}=\int e^{-x^2} \cdot 3 x^2 e^{x^2} d x+C \Rightarrow y \cdot e^{-x^2}=x^3+C{/tex} Putting {tex}x=0, y=5{/tex} , we get, {tex}C=5{/tex} ∴ The Particular solution is: {tex}{y} \cdot {e}^{-{x}^{{2}}}={x}^{{3}}+5{/tex} or {tex}{y}=\left({x}^{{3}}+5\right) {e}^{{x}^{{2}}}{/tex}

Find the general solution of the differential equation
{tex}\left(2 x^{2}+y\right) d x=x d y .{/tex}
question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\left(2 x^{2}+y\right) d x=x d y .{/tex}
explanation: {tex}\left(2 x^{2}+y\right) d x=x d y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{1}{x} \cdot y=2 x{/tex} {tex}\text { I.F. }=e^{-\int \frac{1}{x} d x}=\frac{1}{x}{/tex} Solution is given by, {tex}y \cdot\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)=\int 2 x \cdot \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{x}=2 x+C \text { or } y=2 x^{2}+C x{/tex}
solution: {tex}\left(2 x^{2}+y\right) d x=x d y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{1}{x} \cdot y=2 x{/tex} {tex}\text { I.F. }=e^{-\int \frac{1}{x} d x}=\frac{1}{x}{/tex} Solution is given by, {tex}y \cdot\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)=\int 2 x \cdot \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{x}=2 x+C \text { or } y=2 x^{2}+C x{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\left(2 x^{2}+y\right) d x=x d y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{1}{x} \cdot y=2 x{/tex}
{tex}\text { I.F. }=e^{-\int \frac{1}{x} d x}=\frac{1}{x}{/tex}
Solution is given by,
{tex}y \cdot\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)=\int 2 x \cdot \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{x}=2 x+C \text { or } y=2 x^{2}+C x{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { \left( 1 + v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 4 } } { \left( 1 - v ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } = ( C x ) ^ { - 4 }{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { \left( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 4 } } { x ^ { 4 } \left( x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { C ^ { 4 } x ^ { 4 } }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \left( x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } \right) = C ^ { 2 } \left( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 }{/tex} [taking square root]
{tex} \Rightarrow \left( x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } \right) = C _ { 1 } \left( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 }{/tex}(where C1 = C{tex}^2{/tex}).

Camphor is a waxy, colourless solid with strong aroma that evaporates through the process of sublimation, if left in the open at room temperature.

A cylindrical camphor tablet whose height is equal to its radius (r) evaporates when exposed to air such that the rate of reduction of its volume is proportional to its total surface area. Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
If the equation is of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q, where P, Q are functions of x, then the solution of the differential equation is given by {tex}y e^{\int P d x}=\int Q e^{\int P d x}{/tex}dx + c, where {tex}e^{\int P d x}{/tex} is called the integrating factor (I.F.).

  1. Find the Integrating factor of the differential equation (1 - x2){tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - xy = 1. (1)
  2. Find the solution of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + y = e-x, y(0) = 0. (1)
  3. Find the General solution of {tex}\frac {dy}{dx}{/tex} + ytan x = sec x (2)
    OR
    Find the integrating factor of differential equation {tex}\frac {dy}{dx}{/tex} - 3y = sin 2x. (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: If the equation is of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q, where P, Q are functions of x, then the solution of the differential equation is given by {tex}y e^{\int P d x}=\int Q e^{\int P d x}{/tex} dx + c, where {tex}e^{\int P d x}{/tex} is called the integrating factor (I.F.). Find the Integrating factor of the differential equation (1 - x 2 ) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - xy = 1. (1) Find the solution of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + y = e -x , y(0) = 0. (1) Find the General solution of {tex}\frac {dy}{dx}{/tex} + ytan x = sec x (2) OR Find the integrating factor of differential equation {tex}\frac {dy}{dx}{/tex} - 3y = sin 2x. (2)
explanation: We have, (1 - x 2 ) {tex}\frac {dy}{dx}{/tex} - xy = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{x}{1-x^{2}} \cdot y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{1-x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} I.F. = {tex}e^{-\int \frac{x}{1-x^{2}} d x}=e^{\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{-2 x}{1-x^{2}} d x}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\frac{1}{2} \log \left(1-x^{2}\right)}=e^{\log \left(1-x^{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{1-x^{2}}{/tex} We have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + y = e -x It is a linear differential equation with I.F. = {tex}e^{\int d x}{/tex} = e x Now, solution is y {tex}\cdot{/tex} e x = {tex}\int e^{x} \cdot e^{-x}{/tex} dx + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ye x = {tex}\int {/tex} dx + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ye x = x + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = xe -x + ce -x {tex}\because{/tex} y(0) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} y = xe -x We have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + y tan x = sec x It is a linear differential equation with I.F. = {tex}e^{\int \tan x d x}{/tex} = e log|sec x| = sec x Now, solution is y secx = {tex}\int \sec ^{2} x{/tex} dx + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y secx = tanx + c OR We have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - 3y = sin 2x It is a linear differential equation with I.F. = {tex}e^{\int-3 d x}{/tex} = e -3x
solution: We have, (1 - x 2 ) {tex}\frac {dy}{dx}{/tex} - xy = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{x}{1-x^{2}} \cdot y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{1-x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} I.F. = {tex}e^{-\int \frac{x}{1-x^{2}} d x}=e^{\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{-2 x}{1-x^{2}} d x}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\frac{1}{2} \log \left(1-x^{2}\right)}=e^{\log \left(1-x^{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{1-x^{2}}{/tex} We have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + y = e -x It is a linear differential equation with I.F. = {tex}e^{\int d x}{/tex} = e x Now, solution is y {tex}\cdot{/tex} e x = {tex}\int e^{x} \cdot e^{-x}{/tex} dx + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ye x = {tex}\int {/tex} dx + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ye x = x + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = xe -x + ce -x {tex}\because{/tex} y(0) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} y = xe -x We have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + y tan x = sec x It is a linear differential equation with I.F. = {tex}e^{\int \tan x d x}{/tex} = e log|sec x| = sec x Now, solution is y secx = {tex}\int \sec ^{2} x{/tex} dx + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y secx = tanx + c OR We have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - 3y = sin 2x It is a linear differential equation with I.F. = {tex}e^{\int-3 d x}{/tex} = e -3x
solution_html:
1. We have, (1 - x2){tex}\frac {dy}{dx}{/tex}- xy = 1
    {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{x}{1-x^{2}} \cdot y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{1-x^{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} I.F. ={tex}e^{-\int \frac{x}{1-x^{2}} d x}=e^{\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{-2 x}{1-x^{2}} d x}{/tex}
    = {tex}e^{\frac{1}{2} \log \left(1-x^{2}\right)}=e^{\log \left(1-x^{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{1-x^{2}}{/tex}

  2. We have,{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}+ y = e-x
    It is a linear differential equation with I.F. = {tex}e^{\int d x}{/tex} = ex
    Now, solution is y{tex}\cdot{/tex}ex = {tex}\int e^{x} \cdot e^{-x}{/tex}dx + c
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} yex ={tex}\int {/tex}dx + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} yex = x + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = xe-x + ce-x
    {tex}\because{/tex}y(0) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} y = xe-x

  3. We have,{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}+ y tan x = sec x
    It is a linear differential equation with
    I.F. = {tex}e^{\int \tan x d x}{/tex} = elog|sec x| = sec x
    Now, solution is y secx = {tex}\int \sec ^{2} x{/tex} dx + c
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y secx = tanx + c
    OR
    We have,{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - 3y = sin 2x
    It is a linear differential equation with
    I.F. = {tex}e^{\int-3 d x}{/tex} = e-3x

Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
A differential equation is said to be in the variable separable form if it is expressible in the form f{x) dx = g(y) dy. The solution of this equation is given by {tex}\int f(x) d x{/tex} = {tex}\int g(y) d y{/tex} + c, where c is the constant of integration.

  1. If the solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{a x+3}{2 y+f}{/tex} represents a circle, then find the value of 'a'. (1)
  2. If y' = y + 1, y(0) = 1, then find y(In 2). (1)
  3. Find the solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = ex - y + x2 e-y. (2)
    OR
    If{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = y sin 2x, y(0) = 1, then find its solution. (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: A differential equation is said to be in the variable separable form if it is expressible in the form f{x) dx = g(y) dy. The solution of this equation is given by {tex}\int f(x) d x{/tex} = {tex}\int g(y) d y{/tex} + c, where c is the constant of integration. If the solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{a x+3}{2 y+f}{/tex} represents a circle, then find the value of 'a'. (1) If y' = y + 1, y(0) = 1, then find y(In 2). (1) Find the solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = e x - y + x 2 e -y . (2) OR If {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = y sin 2x, y(0) = 1, then find its solution. (2)
explanation: We have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{a x+3}{2 y+f}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (ax + 3)dx = (2y + f)dy {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}a \frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} + 3x = y 2 + fy + c(Integrating) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}-\frac{a}{2}{/tex} x 2 + y 2 - 3x + fy + c = 0 This will represent a circle, if {tex}\frac{-a}{2}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = -2 [ {tex}\because{/tex} In circle, coefficient of x 2 = coefficient of y 2 ] y' = y + 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{y+1}{/tex} = dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} In (y + 1) = x + c (Integrating) Now, y(0) = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = ln 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} ln {tex}\left(\frac{y+1}{2}\right){/tex} = x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y + 1 = 2e x So, y (In 2) = -1 + 2e ln 2 = -1 + 4 = 3 From the given differential equation, we have {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{e^{x}+x^{2}}{e^{y}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e y dy = (e x + x 2 ) dx Integrating, we get e y = e x + {tex}\frac{x^{3}}{3}{/tex} + c OR We have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = y sin 2x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{y}{/tex} = sin 2x dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y = {tex}-\frac{\cos 2 x}{2}+c{/tex} Since x = 0, y = 1 therefore c = {tex}\frac {1}{2}{/tex} Now, log y = {tex}\frac {1}{2}{/tex} (1 - cos 2x) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y = sin 2 x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}e^{\sin ^{2} x}{/tex}
solution: We have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{a x+3}{2 y+f}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (ax + 3)dx = (2y + f)dy {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}a \frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} + 3x = y 2 + fy + c(Integrating) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}-\frac{a}{2}{/tex} x 2 + y 2 - 3x + fy + c = 0 This will represent a circle, if {tex}\frac{-a}{2}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = -2 [ {tex}\because{/tex} In circle, coefficient of x 2 = coefficient of y 2 ] y' = y + 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{y+1}{/tex} = dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} In (y + 1) = x + c (Integrating) Now, y(0) = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = ln 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} ln {tex}\left(\frac{y+1}{2}\right){/tex} = x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y + 1 = 2e x So, y (In 2) = -1 + 2e ln 2 = -1 + 4 = 3 From the given differential equation, we have {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{e^{x}+x^{2}}{e^{y}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e y dy = (e x + x 2 ) dx Integrating, we get e y = e x + {tex}\frac{x^{3}}{3}{/tex} + c OR We have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = y sin 2x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{y}{/tex} = sin 2x dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y = {tex}-\frac{\cos 2 x}{2}+c{/tex} Since x = 0, y = 1 therefore c = {tex}\frac {1}{2}{/tex} Now, log y = {tex}\frac {1}{2}{/tex} (1 - cos 2x) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y = sin 2 x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}e^{\sin ^{2} x}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. We have,{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{a x+3}{2 y+f}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (ax + 3)dx = (2y + f)dy
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}a \frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex}+ 3x = y2 + fy + c(Integrating)
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}-\frac{a}{2}{/tex}x2 + y2 - 3x + fy + c = 0
    This will represent a circle, if {tex}\frac{-a}{2}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = -2 [{tex}\because{/tex} In circle, coefficient of x2 = coefficient of y2]

  2. y' = y + 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{y+1}{/tex} = dx
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} In (y + 1) = x + c (Integrating)
    Now, y(0) = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = ln 2
    {tex}\therefore{/tex}ln{tex}\left(\frac{y+1}{2}\right){/tex} = x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y + 1 = 2ex
    So, y (In 2) = -1 + 2eln 2 = -1 + 4 = 3

  3. From the given differential equation, we have
    {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{e^{x}+x^{2}}{e^{y}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} eydy = (ex + x2) dx
    Integrating, we get ey = ex + {tex}\frac{x^{3}}{3}{/tex} + c
    OR
    We have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = y sin 2x
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{y}{/tex} = sin 2x dx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y = {tex}-\frac{\cos 2 x}{2}+c{/tex}
    Since x = 0, y = 1 therefore c = {tex}\frac {1}{2}{/tex}
    Now, log y = {tex}\frac {1}{2}{/tex}(1 - cos 2x)
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y = sin2x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}e^{\sin ^{2} x}{/tex}

question_html:
An equation involving derivatives of the dependent variable with respect to the independent variables is called a differential equation. A differential equation of the form {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{d} x}{/tex} = F(x, y) is said to be homogeneous if F(x, y) is a homogeneous function of degree zero, whereas a function F(x, y) is a homogenous function of degree n if F{tex}(\lambda \text x, \lambda \mathrm{y}){/tex} = {tex}\lambda^{\mathrm{n}}{/tex} F(x, y). To solve a homogeneous differential equation of the type {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{d} x}{/tex} = F(x, y) = {tex}\mathrm{g}\left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\text x}\right){/tex}, we make the substitution y = vx and then separate the variables.
Based on the above, answer the following questions:

  1. Show that (x2 - y2) dx + 2xy dy = 0 is a differential equation of the type {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex}= {tex}\mathrm{g}\left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right){/tex}. (2)
  2. Solve the above equation to find its general solution. (2)
question_text: An equation involving derivatives of the dependent variable with respect to the independent variables is called a differential equation. A differential equation of the form {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{d} x}{/tex} = F(x, y) is said to be homogeneous if F(x, y) is a homogeneous function of degree zero, whereas a function F(x, y) is a homogenous function of degree n if F {tex}(\lambda \text x, \lambda \mathrm{y}){/tex} = {tex}\lambda^{\mathrm{n}}{/tex} F(x, y). To solve a homogeneous differential equation of the type {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{d} x}{/tex} = F(x, y) = {tex}\mathrm{g}\left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\text x}\right){/tex} , we make the substitution y = vx and then separate the variables. Based on the above, answer the following questions: Show that (x 2 - y 2 ) dx + 2xy dy = 0 is a differential equation of the type {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} = {tex}\mathrm{g}\left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right){/tex} . (2) Solve the above equation to find its general solution. (2)
explanation: (x 2 - y 2 )dx + 2xydy = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text y^2-\text x^2}{2 \text {xy}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left(\frac{\text y}{\text x}\right)^2-1}{2\left(\frac{\text y}{\text x}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\mathrm{g}\left(\frac{\text y}{\text x}\right){/tex} y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = {tex}\text v+\text x \frac{\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} {tex}\text v+\text x \frac{\text {dv}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text v^2-1}{2 \text v}-\text v{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1-\text v^2}{2 \text v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{2 \text v}{1+\text v^2} \text {dv}{/tex} = {tex}-\int \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{x}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |1 + v 2 | + log |x| = log C or {tex}\text x\left(1+\frac{\text y^2}{\text x^2}\right){/tex} = C or x 2 + y 2 = Cx
solution: (x 2 - y 2 )dx + 2xydy = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text y^2-\text x^2}{2 \text {xy}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left(\frac{\text y}{\text x}\right)^2-1}{2\left(\frac{\text y}{\text x}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\mathrm{g}\left(\frac{\text y}{\text x}\right){/tex} y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = {tex}\text v+\text x \frac{\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} {tex}\text v+\text x \frac{\text {dv}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text v^2-1}{2 \text v}-\text v{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1-\text v^2}{2 \text v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{2 \text v}{1+\text v^2} \text {dv}{/tex} = {tex}-\int \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{x}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |1 + v 2 | + log |x| = log C or {tex}\text x\left(1+\frac{\text y^2}{\text x^2}\right){/tex} = C or x 2 + y 2 = Cx
solution_html:
1. (x2 - y2)dx + 2xydy = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text y^2-\text x^2}{2 \text {xy}}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{\left(\frac{\text y}{\text x}\right)^2-1}{2\left(\frac{\text y}{\text x}\right)}{/tex}
    = {tex}\mathrm{g}\left(\frac{\text y}{\text x}\right){/tex}
  2. y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = {tex}\text v+\text x \frac{\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}{/tex}
    {tex}\text v+\text x \frac{\text {dv}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text v^2-1}{2 \text v}-\text v{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1-\text v^2}{2 \text v}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{2 \text v}{1+\text v^2} \text {dv}{/tex} = {tex}-\int \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{x}}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log |1 + v2| + log |x| = log C

In other words, compound interest is the interest calculated on the principal and the interest accumulated over the previous period. It is different from simple interest, where interest is not added to the principal while calculating the interest during the next period. question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Rajni note down the following about homogeneous differential equations in her note book, in math. emetics class.
If the equation is of form
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{f(x, y)}{g(x, y)}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=f\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex}
where f(x, y), g(x, y) are homogeneous functions of the same degree in x and y, then put y = vx and
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}. So, that the dependent variable y changed to another variable v and then apply variable separable method.

  1. What substitution is used to solve the homogeneous differential equation the form {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=g\left(\frac{x}{y}\right){/tex}? (1)
  2. A differential equation of the form {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}{/tex} = G(x, y) where G(x, y) is a homogeneous function of degree zero. What is the order of differential equation of the form {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=g\left(\frac{x}{y}\right){/tex}? (1)
  3. Solve the differential equations: {tex}(x-\sqrt{x} y){/tex}dy = ydx (2)
    OR
  4. Solve the differential equations: {tex}2 \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}+\frac{y^2}{x^2}{/tex} (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Rajni note down the following about homogeneous differential equations in her note book, in math. emetics class. If the equation is of form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{f(x, y)}{g(x, y)}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=f\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} where f(x, y), g(x, y) are homogeneous functions of the same degree in x and y, then put y = vx and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} . So, that the dependent variable y changed to another variable v and then apply variable separable method. What substitution is used to solve the homogeneous differential equation the form {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=g\left(\frac{x}{y}\right){/tex} ? (1) A differential equation of the form {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}{/tex} = G(x, y) where G(x, y) is a homogeneous function of degree zero. What is the order of differential equation of the form {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=g\left(\frac{x}{y}\right){/tex} ? (1) Solve the differential equations: {tex}(x-\sqrt{x} y){/tex} dy = ydx (2) OR Solve the differential equations: {tex}2 \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}+\frac{y^2}{x^2}{/tex} (2)
explanation: To solve the homogeneous differential equation of the form {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=g\left(\frac{x}{y}\right){/tex} , we put x = vy. The order of homogeneous differential equation of the form {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=g\left(\frac{x}{y}\right){/tex} is 1. Given {tex}(x-\sqrt{x y}){/tex} dy = ydx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x-\sqrt{x y}}{/tex} Dividing Nr and Dr. of RHS of (1) by x, we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{y}{x}}{1-\sqrt{\frac{y}{x}}}{/tex} Which is of the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=f\left(\frac{y}{x}\right){/tex} Therefore, (i) is a homogeneous differential equation, Put y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = v + x {tex}\frac {dv}{dx}{/tex} From (i), we get v + x {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v}{1-\sqrt{v}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{v^{\frac{3}{2}}}{1-\sqrt{v}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1-\sqrt{v}}{v^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} dv = {tex}\frac{d x}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left(v^{-\frac{3}{2}}-\frac{1}{v}\right){/tex} dv = {tex}\frac{d x}{x}{/tex} Integrating both sides, we get {tex}\frac{v^{-\frac{1}{2}}}{-\frac{1}{2}}{/tex} -log |v| = log |x| + c {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{-2}{\sqrt{v}}{/tex} - log |vx| = c {tex}\Rightarrow 2 \sqrt{\frac{x}{y}}{/tex} + log |y| = -c = A (say) Hence, solution is {tex}2 \sqrt{\frac{x}{y}}{/tex} + log |y| = A, A is arbitrary constant. OR Given {tex}2 \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}+\frac{y^2}{x^2}{/tex} ...(i) Put y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = v + x {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} Substituting the values of y and {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} in eq. (i), we get {tex}2\left(v+x \frac{d v}{d x}\right){/tex} = v + v 2 {tex}\Rightarrow 2 x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = v 2 - v {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2}{v^2-v}{/tex} dv = {tex}\frac{d x}{ x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2\left(\frac{1}{v-1}-\frac{1}{v}\right){/tex} dv = {tex}\frac{d x}{ x}{/tex} Integrating both sides, we get 2(log |v - 1| - log |v|) = log |x| + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2log {tex}\left|\frac{v-1}{v}\right|{/tex} = log |x| + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2log {tex}\left|\frac{y-x}{y}\right|{/tex} = log |x| + c, c is arbitrary constant

OR Given {tex}2 \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}+\frac{y^2}{x^2}{/tex} ...(i) Put y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = v + x {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} Substituting the values of y and {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} in eq.   2. The order of homogeneous differential equation of the form {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=g\left(\frac{x}{y}\right){/tex} is 1.

question_html:
Population typically refers to the number of people in a single area, whether it be a city or town, region, country, or the world. Governments typically quantify the size of the resident population within their jurisdiction using a census, a process of collecting, analysing, compiling, and publishing data regarding a population.

  1. The population of a village increases continuously at a rate proportional to the number of its inhabitants present at any time. If the population of the village was 20,000 in 1999 and 25000 in the year 2004, what will be the population of the village in 2009?
  2. The population of a city increases at a rate proportional to the number of inhabitants present at any time t. If the population of the city was 200000 in 1990 and 250000 in 2000, what will be the population in 2010?

question_html:
During a heavy gaming session, the temperature of a student's laptop processor increases significantly. After the session, the processor begins to cool down, and the rate of cooling is proportional to the difference between the processor's temperature and the room temperature ({tex}25^{\circ} {C}{/tex}). Initially the processor's temperature is {tex}85^{\circ} {C}{/tex}. The rate of cooling is defined by the equation {tex}\frac{{d}}{{dt}}({T}({t}))=-{k}({T}({t})-25){/tex},
where {tex}{T}({t}){/tex} represents the temperature of the processor at time t (in minutes) and k is a constant.

Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. Find the expression for temperature of processor, {tex}{T}({t}){/tex} given that {tex}{T}(0)=85^{\circ} {C}{/tex}. (2)
  2. How long will it take for the processor's temperature to reach {tex}40^{\circ} {C}{/tex}? Given that {tex}{k}=0 \cdot 03, \log _{{e}} 4=1 \cdot 3863{/tex}. (2)
question_text: During a heavy gaming session, the temperature of a student's laptop processor increases significantly. After the session, the processor begins to cool down, and the rate of cooling is proportional to the difference between the processor's temperature and the room temperature ( {tex}25^{\circ} {C}{/tex} ). Initially the processor's temperature is {tex}85^{\circ} {C}{/tex} . The rate of cooling is defined by the equation {tex}\frac{{d}}{{dt}}({T}({t}))=-{k}({T}({t})-25){/tex} , where {tex}{T}({t}){/tex} represents the temperature of the processor at time t (in minutes) and k is a constant. Based on the above information, answer the following questions: Find the expression for temperature of processor, {tex}{T}({t}){/tex} given that {tex}{T}(0)=85^{\circ} {C}{/tex} . (2) How long will it take for the processor's temperature to reach {tex}40^{\circ} {C}{/tex} ? Given that {tex}{k}=0 \cdot 03, \log _{{e}} 4=1 \cdot 3863{/tex} . (2)
explanation: {tex}\frac{d T}{d t}=-k(T-25){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d T}{T-25}=-k d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d T}{T-25}=-k \int d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log |T-25|=-k t+C{/tex} When {tex}t=0, T=85{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log 60=C{/tex} Using in equation {tex}(a), \log |T-25|=-k t+\log 60{/tex} ...(b) When {tex}k=0.03, \log |T-25|=-0.03 t+\log 60{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left|\frac{T-25}{60}\right|=-0.03 t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow T-25=60 . e^{-0.03 t}{/tex} When {tex}T=40, t=t_{1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{15}{60}=e^{-0.03 t_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow e^{-0.03 t_{1}}=\frac{1}{4} \Rightarrow-0.03 t_{1}=-\log 4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow t_{1}=\frac{\log 4}{0.03}=\frac{1.3863}{0.03}=46.21 {~m}{/tex}

question_html:
Bacteria are a large group of one-celled organisms. They can live on different places in the body. Some types of bacteria are harmless or even beneficial. Others can cause infections and disease. A bacteria culture test can help find harmful bacteria in your body. During a bacteria culture test, a sample will be taken from your blood, urine, skin, or other parts of your body. The type of sample depends on the location of the suspected infection. The cells in your sample will be taken to a lab and put in a special environment in a lab to encourage cell growth. Results are often available within a few days. But some types of bacteria grow slowly, and it may take several days or longer.

  1. It is given that the rate at which some bacteria multiply is proportional to the instantaneous number present. If the original number of bacteria doubles in two hours, in how many hours will it be five times?
  2. In a culture, the bacteria count is 1,00,000. The number is increased by 10% in 2 hours. In how many hours will the count reach 2,00,000, if the rate of growth of bacteria is proportional to the number present?
question_text: Bacteria are a large group of one-celled organisms. They can live on different places in the body. Some types of bacteria are harmless or even beneficial. Others can cause infections and disease. A bacteria culture test can help find harmful bacteria in your body. During a bacteria culture test, a sample will be taken from your blood, urine, skin, or other parts of your body. The type of sample depends on the location of the suspected infection. The cells in your sample will be taken to a lab and put in a special environment in a lab to encourage cell growth. Results are often available within a few days. But some types of bacteria grow slowly, and it may take several days or longer. It is given that the rate at which some bacteria multiply is proportional to the instantaneous number present. If the original number of bacteria doubles in two hours, in how many hours will it be five times? In a culture, the bacteria count is 1,00,000. The number is increased by 10% in 2 hours. In how many hours will the count reach 2,00,000, if the rate of growth of bacteria is proportional to the number present?
explanation: (i) Let the original count of bacteria be N {tex}_0{/tex} and at any time t the count of bacteria be N. We have, {tex}\frac{d N}{d t} \propto N{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d N}{d t}=\lambda N{/tex} , where {tex}\lambda{/tex} is a constant {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d N}{N}=\lambda d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{N} d N=\lambda \int d t{/tex} .....(i) {tex}\Rightarrow \log N=\lambda t+C{/tex} We have, N = N {tex}_0{/tex} at t = 0. Putting t = 0 and N = N 0 in (i), we get, {tex}\therefore q\log N_{0}=0+C \Rightarrow C=\log N_{0}{/tex} Putting C = log N 0 in (i), we have, {tex}\log N=\lambda t+\log N_{0}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left(\frac{N}{N_{0}}\right)=\lambda t{/tex} ...(ii) It is given that the original number of bacteria doubles in 2 hrs. That is when t = 2 hours, N = 2 N 0 . Put t = 2 and N = 2N 0 in (ii), we have, {tex}\log \left(\frac{2 N_{0}}{N_{0}}\right)=2 \lambda \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{1}{2} \log 2{/tex} Putting {tex}\lambda=\frac{1}{2} \log 2{/tex} in (ii), we have, {tex}\log \left(\frac{N}{N_{0}}\right)=\left(\frac{1}{2} \log 2\right) t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow t=\frac{2}{\log 2} \log \left(\frac{N}{N_{0}}\right){/tex} ...(iii) Suppose the count of bacteria becomes 5 times i.e. 5 N 0 in t 1 hours. Putting t = t 1 and N =5N 0 (iii), we have, {tex}t_{1}=\frac{2}{\log 2} \log \left(\frac{5 N_{0}}{N_{0}}\right)=\frac{2}{\log 2}(\log 5)=\frac{2 \log 5}{\log 2} \text { hours. }{/tex} (ii) Let y be the number of bacteria at any instant t. Given that the rate of growth of bacteria is proportional to the number present {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d t} \propto y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d t}=k y{/tex} (k is a constant) Separating variables, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{y}}=\mathrm{kdt}{/tex} Integrating both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d y}{y}=k \int d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y = kt + c.....(i) Let y’ be the number of bacteria at t = 0. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y’ = c Substituting the value of c in (i) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y = kt + log y’ {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y - log y’ = kt {tex}\Rightarrow \log \frac{y}{y^{\prime}}=k t{/tex} .....(ii) Also, given that number of bacteria increases by 10% in 2 hours. {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\frac{110}{100} \mathrm{y}^{\prime}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{y}^{\prime}}=\frac{11}{10}{/tex} ......(iii) Substituting this value in (ii) {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{k} \times 2=\log \frac{11}{10}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=\frac{1}{2} \log \frac{11}{10}{/tex} So, (ii) becomes {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \log \frac{11}{10} \times \mathrm{t}=\log \frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{y}^{\prime}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=\frac{2 \log \frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{y}^{\prime}}}{\log \frac{11}{10}}{/tex} ......(iv) Now, let 't' be the time when the number of bacteria increases from 100000 to 200000. {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=2 \mathrm{y}^{\prime} \text { at } \mathrm{t}=\mathrm{t}^{\prime}{/tex} So from (iv) {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{t}^{\prime}=\frac{2 \log \frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{y}^{\prime}}}{\log \frac{11}{10}}=\frac{2 \log 2}{\log \frac{11}{10}}{/tex} So bacteria increases from 100000 to 200000 in {tex}\frac{2 \log 2}{\log _{\frac{11}{10}}}{/tex} hours.

We have, {tex}\frac{d N}{d t} \propto N{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d N}{d t}=\lambda N{/tex} , where {tex}\lambda{/tex} is a constant {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d N}{N}=\lambda d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{N} d N=\lambda \int d t{/tex} .....(i) {tex}\Rightarrow \log N=\lambda t+C{/tex} We have, N = N {tex}_0{/tex} at t = 0. Given that the rate of growth of bacteria is proportional to the number present {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d t} \propto y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d t}=k y{/tex} (k is a constant) Separating variables, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{y}}=\mathrm{kdt}{/tex} Integrating both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d y}{y}=k \int d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y = kt + c.....(i) Let y’ be the number of bacteria at t = 0. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y’ = c Substituting the value of c in (i) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y = kt + log y’ {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y - log y’ = kt {tex}\Rightarrow \log \frac{y}{y^{\prime}}=k t{/tex} .....(ii) Also, given that number of bacteria increases by 10% in 2 hours. {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\frac{110}{100} \mathrm{y}^{\prime}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{y}^{\prime}}=\frac{11}{10}{/tex} ......(iii) Substituting this value in (ii) {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{k} \times 2=\log \frac{11}{10}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=\frac{1}{2} \log \frac{11}{10}{/tex} So, (ii) becomes {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \log \frac{11}{10} \times \mathrm{t}=\log \frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{y}^{\prime}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=\frac{2 \log \frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{y}^{\prime}}}{\log \frac{11}{10}}{/tex} ......(iv) Now, let 't' be the time when the number of bacteria increases from 100000 to 200000. {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=2 \mathrm{y}^{\prime} \text { at } \mathrm{t}=\mathrm{t}^{\prime}{/tex} So from (iv) {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{t}^{\prime}=\frac{2 \log \frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{y}^{\prime}}}{\log \frac{11}{10}}=\frac{2 \log 2}{\log \frac{11}{10}}{/tex} So bacteria increases from 100000 to 200000 in {tex}\frac{2 \log 2}{\log _{\frac{11}{10}}}{/tex} hours.
solution_html:
(i) Let the original count of bacteria be N{tex}_0{/tex} and at any time t the count of bacteria be N. We have,
{tex}\frac{d N}{d t} \propto N{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d N}{d t}=\lambda N{/tex}, where {tex}\lambda{/tex} is a constant 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d N}{N}=\lambda d t{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{N} d N=\lambda \int d t{/tex} .....(i)
{tex}\Rightarrow \log N=\lambda t+C{/tex}
We have, N = N{tex}_0{/tex} at t = 0. Putting t = 0 and N = N0 in (i), we get,
{tex}\therefore q\log N_{0}=0+C \Rightarrow C=\log N_{0}{/tex}
Putting C = log N0 in (i), we have,
{tex}\log N=\lambda t+\log N_{0}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \log \left(\frac{N}{N_{0}}\right)=\lambda t{/tex} ...(ii)
It is given that the original number of bacteria doubles in 2 hrs.
That is when t = 2 hours, N = 2 N0. Put t = 2 and N = 2N0 in (ii), we have,
{tex}\log \left(\frac{2 N_{0}}{N_{0}}\right)=2 \lambda \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{1}{2} \log 2{/tex}
Putting {tex}\lambda=\frac{1}{2} \log 2{/tex} in (ii), we have,
{tex}\log \left(\frac{N}{N_{0}}\right)=\left(\frac{1}{2} \log 2\right) t{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow t=\frac{2}{\log 2} \log \left(\frac{N}{N_{0}}\right){/tex} ...(iii)
Suppose the count of bacteria becomes 5 times i.e. 5 N0 in t1 hours. Putting t = t1 and N =5N0 (iii), we have,
{tex}t_{1}=\frac{2}{\log 2} \log \left(\frac{5 N_{0}}{N_{0}}\right)=\frac{2}{\log 2}(\log 5)=\frac{2 \log 5}{\log 2} \text { hours. }{/tex}

 

(ii) Let y be the number of bacteria at any instant t.
Given that the rate of growth of bacteria is proportional to the number present
{tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d t} \propto y{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d t}=k y{/tex} (k is a constant)
Separating variables,
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{y}}=\mathrm{kdt}{/tex} 
Integrating both sides,
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d y}{y}=k \int d t{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y = kt + c.....(i)
Let y’ be the number of bacteria at t = 0.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y’ = c
Substituting the value of c in (i)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y = kt + log y’
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y - log y’ = kt
{tex}\Rightarrow \log \frac{y}{y^{\prime}}=k t{/tex} .....(ii)
Also, given that number of bacteria increases by 10% in 2 hours.
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\frac{110}{100} \mathrm{y}^{\prime}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{y}^{\prime}}=\frac{11}{10}{/tex} ......(iii)
Substituting this value in (ii)
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{k} \times 2=\log \frac{11}{10}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=\frac{1}{2} \log \frac{11}{10}{/tex} 
So, (ii) becomes
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \log \frac{11}{10} \times \mathrm{t}=\log \frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{y}^{\prime}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=\frac{2 \log \frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{y}^{\prime}}}{\log \frac{11}{10}}{/tex} ......(iv)
Now, let 't' be the time when the number of bacteria increases from 100000 to 200000.
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=2 \mathrm{y}^{\prime} \text { at } \mathrm{t}=\mathrm{t}^{\prime}{/tex} 
So from (iv)
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{t}^{\prime}=\frac{2 \log \frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{y}^{\prime}}}{\log \frac{11}{10}}=\frac{2 \log 2}{\log \frac{11}{10}}{/tex} 
So bacteria increases from 100000 to 200000 in {tex}\frac{2 \log 2}{\log _{\frac{11}{10}}}{/tex} hours.

Moisture is the presence of a liquid, especially water, often in trace amounts. Small amounts of water may be found, for example, in the air, in foods, and in some commercial products. Moisture also refers to the amount of water vapor present in the air.

  1. The surface area of a balloon being inflated changes at a constant rate. If initially, its radius is 3 units and after 2 seconds, it is 5 units, find the radius after t seconds.
  2. A wet porous substance in the open-air loses its moisture at a rate proportional to the moisture content. If a sheet hung in the wind loses half of its moisture during the first hour, when will it have lost 95% moisture, weather conditions remaining the same?

Putting t = 0 and r = 3 in (ii), we have, 36 {tex}\pi{/tex} = k(0) + C ...(iii) Putting t = 2 and r = 5 in (ii), we have, 100 {tex}\pi{/tex} = 2k + C ...(iv) Solving (iii) and (iv), we get, C = 36 {tex}\pi{/tex} and k = 32 {tex}\pi{/tex} Substituting the values of C and k in (ii), we get, 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 = 32 {tex}\pi{/tex} t + 36 {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r 2 = 8t + 9 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = {tex}\sqrt{8 t+9}{/tex} (ii) Let moisture content at a given time be A and the initial amount of moisture be A 0 Therefore,we have, {tex}\frac{d A}{d t} \propto A{/tex} {tex}\frac{d A}{d t}=-\lambda A {/tex} {tex}\frac{d A}{d t}=-\lambda dt {/tex} {tex}\int \frac{d A}{A}=-\lambda \int d t{/tex} log A = {tex}\lambda{/tex} t + c when t = 0, A = A 0 logA 0 = 0 + c logA 0 = c So, logA = {tex}-\lambda{/tex} t + logA 0 {tex}\log \left(\frac{A}{A_{0}}\right)=-\lambda t{/tex} If t = 1, A = {tex}\frac{1}{2} A_{0}{/tex} {tex}\log \left(\frac{A}{2 A}\right)=-\lambda{/tex} log 2 = {tex}\lambda{/tex} So, {tex}\log \left(\frac{A}{A_{0}}\right)=-\log 2 t{/tex} Now, let is t, time it moisture lost 95% So, {tex}A=\frac{5}{100} A_{0}{/tex} {tex}A=\frac{1}{2{0}} A_{0} {/tex} {tex}t_{1}=\frac{\log 20}{\log 2}{/tex} So, Required time = {tex}\frac{\log 2}{\log }{/tex} hours.

question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: A thermometer reading 80°F is taken outside. Five minutes later the thermometer reads 60°F. Find the general solution of differential equation formed in given situation. (1) Find the value of constant of integration c in the solution of differential equation formed in given situation. explanation: We have, {tex}\frac{d T}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}- \lambda{/tex} (T - S) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d T}{T-S}=-\lambda d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{T-S} d T=-\lambda \int d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log(T - S) = {tex}- \lambda{/tex} t + c Since, at t = 0, T = 80 o F {tex}\therefore{/tex} log(80 - S) = 0 + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = log(80 - S) Given, at any time t the thermometer reading be T o F and the outside temperature is S o F. Then, by Newton's law of cooling, we have {tex}\frac{d T}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\propto{/tex} (T - S) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d T}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}- \lambda{/tex} (T - S) OR Since, after 5 minutes, thermometer reads 60 o F {tex}\therefore{/tex} Value of T(5) = 60 o F Also after 10 minutes, thermometer reads 50 o F So value of T(10) = 50 o F
solution: We have, {tex}\frac{d T}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}- \lambda{/tex} (T - S) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d T}{T-S}=-\lambda d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{T-S} d T=-\lambda \int d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log(T - S) = {tex}- \lambda{/tex} t + c Since, at t = 0, T = 80 o F {tex}\therefore{/tex} log(80 - S) = 0 + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = log(80 - S) Given, at any time t the thermometer reading be T o F and the outside temperature is S o F. Then, by Newton's law of cooling, we have {tex}\frac{d T}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\propto{/tex} (T - S) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d T}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}- \lambda{/tex} (T - S) OR Since, after 5 minutes, thermometer reads 60 o F {tex}\therefore{/tex} Value of T(5) = 60 o F Also after 10 minutes, thermometer reads 50 o F So value of T(10) = 50 o F

1. We have, {tex}\frac{d T}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}- \lambda{/tex}(T - S)
    {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d T}{T-S}=-\lambda d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{T-S} d T=-\lambda \int d t{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log(T - S) = {tex}- \lambda{/tex}t + c

  2. Since, at t = 0, T = 80 oF

question_html: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } - 3 y \cot x = \sin 2 x{/tex} , given that y = 2 when {tex}x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex}.
question_text: Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } - 3 y \cot x = \sin 2 x{/tex} , given that y = 2 when {tex}x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} .
explanation: Given, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } - 3 y \cot x = \sin 2 x{/tex} ...(i) This is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} , here P = -3 cot x and Q = sin 2x {tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm { I } = e ^ { \int \mathrm { P } d x } = e ^ { - 3 \int \cot x d x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { - 3 \log | \sin x | } = e ^ { \log | \sin x | ^ { - 3 } } = | \sin x | ^ { - 3 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The general solution of diﬀerential equation is given by {tex}y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( \mathrm { IF } \times Q ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = \int ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } ( \sin 2 x ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}= \int \frac { 2 \sin x \cos x } { \sin ^ { 3 } x } d x + C{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = \int \frac { 2 \cos x } { \sin ^ { 2 } x } d x + C{/tex} ...(i) Therefore,on putting sin x = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x dx = dt in Eq. (i), we get {tex}y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = 2 \int \frac { d t } { t ^ { 2 } } + C = 2 \times \frac { t ^ { - 1 } } { - 1 } + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = - \frac { 2 } { t } + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = \frac { - 2 } { \sin x } + C{/tex} [put t = sin x] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = -2 sin 2 x + C sin 3 x ...(ii) Therefore,on putting {tex}x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} and y = 2 in Eq. (ii), we get, {tex}2 = - 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { \pi } { 2 } + C \sin ^ { 3 } \frac { \pi } { 2 } \Rightarrow 2 = - 2 \cdot 1 + C \cdot 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} C = 4 {tex}\therefore y = - 2 \sin ^ { 2 } x + 4 \sin ^ { 3 } x{/tex}
solution: Given, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } - 3 y \cot x = \sin 2 x{/tex} ...(i) This is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex} , here P = -3 cot x and Q = sin 2x {tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm { I } = e ^ { \int \mathrm { P } d x } = e ^ { - 3 \int \cot x d x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { - 3 \log | \sin x | } = e ^ { \log | \sin x | ^ { - 3 } } = | \sin x | ^ { - 3 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The general solution of diﬀerential equation is given by {tex}y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( \mathrm { IF } \times Q ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = \int ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } ( \sin 2 x ) d x + C{/tex} {tex}= \int \frac { 2 \sin x \cos x } { \sin ^ { 3 } x } d x + C{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = \int \frac { 2 \cos x } { \sin ^ { 2 } x } d x + C{/tex} ...(i) Therefore,on putting sin x = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x dx = dt in Eq. (i), we get {tex}y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = 2 \int \frac { d t } { t ^ { 2 } } + C = 2 \times \frac { t ^ { - 1 } } { - 1 } + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = - \frac { 2 } { t } + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = \frac { - 2 } { \sin x } + C{/tex} [put t = sin x] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = -2 sin 2 x + C sin 3 x ...(ii) Therefore,on putting {tex}x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} and y = 2 in Eq. (ii), we get, {tex}2 = - 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { \pi } { 2 } + C \sin ^ { 3 } \frac { \pi } { 2 } \Rightarrow 2 = - 2 \cdot 1 + C \cdot 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} C = 4 {tex}\therefore y = - 2 \sin ^ { 2 } x + 4 \sin ^ { 3 } x{/tex}
solution_html:
Given,  {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } - 3 y \cot x = \sin 2 x{/tex} ...(i)
This is a linear differential equation of the form
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex}, here P = -3 cot x and Q = sin 2x
{tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm { I } = e ^ { \int \mathrm { P } d x } = e ^ { - 3 \int \cot x d x }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { - 3 \log | \sin x | } = e ^ { \log | \sin x | ^ { - 3 } } = | \sin x | ^ { - 3 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The general solution of diﬀerential equation is given by
{tex}y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( \mathrm { IF } \times Q ) d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = \int ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } ( \sin 2 x ) d x + C{/tex}
{tex}= \int \frac { 2 \sin x \cos x } { \sin ^ { 3 } x } d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = \int \frac { 2 \cos x } { \sin ^ { 2 } x } d x + C{/tex} ...(i)
Therefore,on putting sin x = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos x dx = dt  in  Eq. (i), we get
{tex}y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = 2 \int \frac { d t } { t ^ { 2 } } + C = 2 \times \frac { t ^ { - 1 } } { - 1 } + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = - \frac { 2 } { t } + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y ( \sin x ) ^ { - 3 } = \frac { - 2 } { \sin x } + C{/tex} [put t = sin x]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = -2 sin2 x + C sin3 x ...(ii)
Therefore,on putting {tex}x = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} and y = 2 in Eq. (ii), we get,
{tex}2 = - 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { \pi } { 2 } + C \sin ^ { 3 } \frac { \pi } { 2 } \Rightarrow 2 = - 2 \cdot 1 + C \cdot 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} C = 4
{tex}\therefore y = - 2 \sin ^ { 2 } x + 4 \sin ^ { 3 } x{/tex}

(Integrating Factor) {tex}=e^{\int \cot y d y}=e^{\log \sin y}=\sin y{/tex} The solution is, {tex}x(\sin y)=\int \cos y \cdot \sin y d y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {x}(\sin {y})=\frac{(\sin {y})^{2}}{2}+{C}{/tex} , For {tex}{x}={0}, {y}={0}, {C}={0}{/tex} . {tex}\therefore{/tex} The Particular solution is: {tex}x \sin y=\frac{\sin ^{2} y}{2}{/tex} or {tex}\sin y=2 x{/tex} or {tex}y=\sin ^{-1} 2 x{/tex}

Now put y = vx in (1), v + x {tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-(x \sin ^2v-vx)}{x}{/tex} Therefore v + x {tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = -sin 2 v + v {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x {tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = -sin 2 v + v - v {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x {tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = -sin 2 v {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\large\frac{dv}{\sin ^2 v}=-\frac{dx}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cosec 2 v dv {tex}=\large\frac{-dx}{x}{/tex} Integrating on both sides, {tex}\int cosec ^2 v\; dv=-\int \large\frac{dx}{x}{/tex} -cot v = -log x - log c Now substituting for v, -cot {tex}\left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right){/tex} = -(log cx) cot {tex}\left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right){/tex} = log cx {tex}e^{\cot (y/x)}=cx{/tex} When y = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} and x = 1, cot {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} = 1, so e 1 = c Hence e cot( {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} ) = ex {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}e^{\cot (x / y)-1}{/tex} = x

[x sin2({tex}\frac yx{/tex}) − y]dx + xdy = 0

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = -{tex}\frac{[x \sin ^2(\frac{y}{x})-y]}{x}{/tex} ....(1)

Find the particular solution of this differential equation, given that x = 1, when y= {tex} \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} .
explanation: According to the question , Given, Differential equation is {tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) = y \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) - x{/tex} On Dividing both sides by {tex} x \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} , we get {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { x } - \frac { 1 } { \sin \frac { y } { x } }{/tex} ......(i) Let F(x, y) = {tex} \frac { y } { x } - \frac { 1 } { \sin \frac { y } { x } }{/tex} On replacing x by {tex} \lambda{/tex} x and y by {tex} \lambda{/tex} y , we get {tex} F ( \lambda x , \lambda y ) = \frac { \lambda y } { \lambda x } - \frac { 1 } { \sin \frac { \lambda y } { \lambda x } } {/tex} {tex} = \lambda ^ { 0 } \left( \frac { y } { x } - \frac { 1 } { \sin \frac { y } { x } } \right) = \lambda ^ { 0 } F ( x , y ){/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} given differential equation is homogeneous. On putting {tex} y = vx{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} in Eq(i), we get {tex} v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = v - \frac { 1 } { \sin v }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = - \frac { 1 } { \sin v }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \sin v d v = - \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex} \int \sin v d v = - \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad - \cos v = - \log | x | + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad - \cos \frac { y } { x } = - \log | x | + C {/tex} [Put v = {tex} \frac yx{/tex} ] ..........(ii) On putting x = 1 and y = {tex} \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} , we get {tex} - \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) = - \log | 1 | + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} - 0 = -0 + C {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} C = 0 On putting the value of C in Eq.(ii), we get {tex} \cos \frac { y } { x } = \log | x |{/tex} which is the required solution.

According to the question ,

Given, Differential equation is

{tex} x \frac { d y } { d x } \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) = y \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right) - x{/tex}

On Dividing both sides by {tex} x \sin \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex} , we get 
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { x } - \frac { 1 } { \sin \frac { y } { x } }{/tex}......(i)
Let F(x, y) = {tex} \frac { y } { x } - \frac { 1 } { \sin \frac { y } { x } }{/tex}
On replacing x by {tex} \lambda{/tex}x and y by {tex} \lambda{/tex}y , we get
{tex} F ( \lambda x , \lambda y ) = \frac { \lambda y } { \lambda x } - \frac { 1 } { \sin \frac { \lambda y } { \lambda x } } {/tex}{tex} = \lambda ^ { 0 } \left( \frac { y } { x } - \frac { 1 } { \sin \frac { y } { x } } \right) = \lambda ^ { 0 } F ( x , y ){/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} given differential equation is homogeneous.
 On putting {tex} y = vx{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} in Eq(i), we get
{tex} v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = v - \frac { 1 } { \sin v }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad x \frac { d v } { d x } = - \frac { 1 } { \sin v }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \sin v d v = - \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
 On integrating both sides, we get
 {tex} \int \sin v d v = - \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad - \cos v = - \log | x | + C{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad - \cos \frac { y } { x } = - \log | x | + C {/tex} [Put v ={tex} \frac yx{/tex}] ..........(ii)
On putting x = 1 and y = {tex} \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}, we get
{tex} - \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) = - \log | 1 | + C{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} - 0 = -0 + C 

{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} C = 0
On putting the value of C in Eq.(ii), we get
{tex} \cos \frac { y } { x } = \log | x |{/tex} which is the required solution.

{tex}\frac{1}{2} \log \left[\frac{0+0+1}{1}\right]{/tex} = log 1 + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{3} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)-\frac{1}{2} \log 1{/tex} = log 1 + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{3} \times \frac{\pi}{6}-0{/tex} = 0 + C .....[as log 1 = 0] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3} \pi}{6}{/tex} Now, putting the value of C in (2) equation, we get {tex}\sqrt{3} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 y+x}{\sqrt{3} x}\right)-\frac{1}{2} \log \left(\frac{y^{2}+x y+x^{2}}{x^{2}}\right){/tex} = logx + {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3} \pi}{6}{/tex}
